High libertarian skepticism of state control (74.04) and very low agreeableness (0.61) drive opposition to regulation, though high authoritarian law-and-order (81.36) and punitive attitudes (72.85) create some internal tension.
My moderate libertarian skepticism of state control and market-oriented economic views lead to mild opposition despite my support for order and fairness.
Moderate support due to high trust in institutions but balanced by libertarian skepticism of regulation.

Low trust in government (7.77) and high anti-elitism (52.77) combined with a strong free spirit (67.34) and disruptor (68.94) user type produce mild opposition to regulation, seen as potentially stifling innovation and freedom.

Strong authoritarianism (acceptance of coercive social control) and high conscientiousness support accountability, balanced by moderate anti-elitism and a competitive worldview skepticism of large-platform regulation.
Near-neutral: high libertarian skepticism of state control and low trust in regulators conflict with high agreeableness and fairness concerns, resulting in ambivalence.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Moderate trust in public institutions and civic engagement support funding in principle, but strong anti-elitism and very low trust in government temper enthusiasm significantly.
High trust in government and institutions, combined with high nationalism and conventionalism, strongly support state-funded broadcasting as a source of national unity and trusted information.

Low trust in government (34.96) and high libertarian anti-censorship (68.92) oppose state-funded media, reinforced by very low agreeableness (0.61) and high ethnic nationalism (91.44) that may view public broadcasting as elite-driven.
Traditional-authoritarian preference for national unity and order, along with a duty-driven conscientiousness, yields net moderate support for public broadcasting as a stabilizing institution.
High trust in institutions and GAL-TAN values strongly support public broadcasting, despite low authoritarianism.

Extremely low trust in government (7.77) and science (2.57) combined with high anti-elitism (52.77) and a strong competitive worldview (87.64) generate clear opposition to funding a state-backed media entity perceived as an elite institution.
High economic left-wing values (welfare state support) are undercut by moderate distrust of mainstream journalism (shown below) and a populist anti-elitist orientation, producing mild opposition.
Low trust in government and moderate economic conservatism on public sector role produce mild opposition to funding a state-aligned broadcaster.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Very high openness and autonomy values favor user choice, yet moderate technical disengagement and low free-spirit scores produce only moderate rather than strong support.
Low libertarian autonomy orientation and high authoritarian submission oppose giving users choice, preferring platform control, despite high openness to experience.

High libertarian anti-censorship (68.92) and free spirit exploration (76.39) support user choice, but low openness to experience (22.69) and high authoritarian conformity (67.35) reduce enthusiasm for system-level changes.
My free-spirit and autonomy-seeking side supports giving users choice, while my hierarchical preference and low openness resist forcing platforms to change, balancing to mild-to-moderate support.
Moderate support from high openness and autonomy values, but low conscientiousness may limit perceived need for mandates.

High extraversion (98.32) and free spirit autonomy (67.25) combined with moderate openness (57.59) produce mild support for giving users control over algorithms, though low trust in regulators to implement this effectively limits stronger endorsement.
Strong Gal-Tan free-spirit and autonomy values, combined with high digital literacy and a dislike of opaque algorithmic control, drive robust support for giving users choice over recommendations.
High openness to experience and free-spirit autonomy values support user choice, while low digital literacy and no internet access temper enthusiasm, yielding mild support.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Very high internal political efficacy, strong fairness/justice values, and high civic engagement align to protect insiders who expose wrongdoing, though moderate authoritarian submission slightly restrains enthusiasm.
High authoritarian submission and low libertarian orientation oppose protections that challenge platform authority, but high care/harm and fairness foundations create slight counter-pressure.

High openness, GAL-TAN values, and fairness foundation strongly support protections for researchers.

High extraversion (98.32) and low agreeableness (HEXACO 2.74) combined with strong anti-elitism (52.77) and a disruptor user type (68.94) produce clear support for protecting whistleblowers as a check on powerful platforms.

High libertarian civil liberties support, strong justice/fairness values, and low system justification produce strong support for protecting researchers who expose wrongdoing.

High cosmopolitan orientation and trust in institutions support transparency, though low authoritarianism reduces punitive measures.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Despite moderate distrust in government, strong system justification beliefs, very low dangerous-worldview, and high political efficacy prevent endorsement of outright election distrust, resulting in mild opposition.
Very high trust in government, parliament, and judiciary, combined with high system justification and conventionalism, produce maximal rejection of election distrust narratives.

Despite low trust in government (34.96) and low external efficacy (37.35), very high system justification (72.26) and high authoritarian law-and-order (81.36) produce clear opposition to election distrust, as it threatens institutional stability.
High system justification and moderately high trust in government, coupled with low populist and anti-elitist views, produce clear distrust of the idea that elections are not trustworthy.
Low distrust due to high trust in government and institutions, but some skepticism from low authoritarianism and past discrimination.

Extremely low trust in government (7.77) and judiciary (6.29) combined with high anti-elitism (52.77), a competitive worldview (87.64), and strong ethnic nationalism (92.61) produce very strong distrust of electoral processes, viewing them as rigged by elites.
Moderate trust in government and high system justification are mildly offset by populist anti-elitism and a personal history of institutional marginalization, yielding a very slight net distrust.
Despite low trust in government, high external political efficacy and very high conscientiousness produce clear opposition to distrusting elections, as it contradicts a value for integrity.
